heading: MODULO SEDICESIMO
card_summary: Chunk 132 of De Harmonia Mundi presents "Modulo Sedicesimo," a theological treatise on Peace (Pax) in three concentus. Zorzi opens with Augustine's definition of peace as "tranquillitas ordinis" and elaborates a graduated hierarchy of peace from bodily health through rational-soul unity to divine concord. He argues that only God, as the cosmic Accordatore (tuner of instruments), can harmonize the human composite of body, soul, animal spirit, and rational will. Psalm 133 ("Ecce quam bonum") is expounded via Aaron's anointing oil and the dew of Hermon as Kabbalistic figures of divine influx, with the Hebrew word for dew, tal, decoded numerologically as the cognomen of the hidden divine Name. The chapter closes by tracing the ascent from peace to blessing, from blessing to life, and from life to union with the divine, which Zorzi promises to treat in the following section.
essay:
Now I'll expand the essay from ~1200 words to 2000-4000 words by adding deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with sources, comprehensive explanations of concepts, fuller theological treatment, and connections to broader themes. I'll preserve all blockquotes, citations, and formatting.

# De Harmonia Mundi: Modulo Sedicesimo — An Expanded Essay on Pax

## Overview

Chunk 132 constitutes the sixteenth *modulo* of the *Canticum Tertium*, eighth *Tono*, of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Its subject is **Pax** — Peace — expounded in three numbered *concentus* that move from philosophical definition, through theological source, to contemplative praise, culminating in a Kabbalistic excursus on Psalm 133. The passage is a characteristic Zorzian synthesis: Augustinian moral psychology, Neoplatonic musical cosmology, Pauline pneumatology, liturgical typology, and Christian Kabbalah woven into a continuous argument about the harmonic constitution of the soul and its ascent to God.

The placement of this modulo within the eighth *Tonus* is itself theologically significant. In the scheme of *De Harmonia Mundi*, peace occupies a mediating position: it is neither the beginning of the soul's ascent (that is charity, *caritas*) nor its end (that is union, *unio*), but rather the stable, transparent condition in which all higher contemplative work becomes possible. As Zorzi will later argue, one cannot ascend to union with the divine while the soul remains fragmented, discordant, or prey to passion. Peace is thus simultaneously a gift already conferred upon the righteous and a condition perpetually requiring divine maintenance—a theme that emerges forcefully in the *concentus* on the divine *Accordatore*.

---

## Concentus I: The Definition of Peace (§ 1)

### Augustinian Foundations and the Taxonomy of Order

Zorzi opens with an explicit appeal to **Augustine** (footnote 928), quoting the celebrated definition from *De Civitate Dei* XIX: peace is "la tranquillità dell'ordine" (*tranquillitas ordinis*). This definition, which became axiomatic in medieval and Renaissance theology, represents Augustine's culmination of centuries of Platonic and Stoic thinking about order as the primary good. Augustine writes in the original: "Peace of the body is the ordered concord of its parts; peace of the irrational soul is the ordered repose of the appetites; peace of the rational soul is the ordered harmony of knowledge and action; and peace of body and soul together is the ordered life and health of the living whole." By consciously translating this definition, Zorzi situates himself within a genealogy that runs from Plato's *kosmos* through the Stoic *logos spermatikos* to Christian patristic thought.

From this Augustinian anchor, Zorzi derives a **graduated taxonomy of peace** that moves through increasingly spiritual registers, each building upon the prior:

- **Peace of the body** (*pax corporis*): the ordered composition of its parts, identified with *salute* (health). This is the lowest peace, governed by the natural concord of the four humors and the proper functioning of the organs. It is the peace of Aristotelian *phronesis* applied to bodily life—right proportion and measure.

- **Peace of the irrational soul** (*pax animae irrationalis*): an ordered, tranquil appetite. Here Zorzi follows the tripartite psychology of Plato's *Republic*, where the appetitive part (ἐπιθυμητικόν) must be brought into obedience to reason. This peace is attained when the bodily desires cease their rebellious clamoring and accept the governance of higher faculties.

- **Peace of body and soul together** (*pax corporis et animae*): harmonious life and concord between the two. This represents the *symphonia* of material and immaterial principles—the classical problem that had preoccupied Neoplatonists from Plotinus onward. For Zorzi, true peace at this level requires that the body not drag the soul into vice through sensuality, nor that the soul exhaust itself in futile attempts to suppress the body's necessary functions.

- **Peace of the rational soul** (*pax rationalis*): ordered knowledge and unified capacity for choice. This is the highest achievable peace in the merely natural order. It consists in the soul's acquisition of wisdom (*sapientia*) and its consequent ability to will the good consistently.

The categorical leap then occurs. The highest earthly peace that Zorzi describes is the "felicissima riunione di due o tre" — the most blessed gathering of two or three — an allusion to Matthew 18:20 ("Ubi sunt duo vel tres congregati in nomine meo, ibi sum in medio eorum"). But Zorzi performs a characteristically inward turn: he reinterprets this gospel promise as the interior concord of the animal faculty with reason, reason with will, and will with reason in mutual feedback. This is no longer political peace (the concordia of citizens) or even familial peace, but the *mystical* peace of internal unification. The very structure of consciousness becomes, in this reading, a gathering where Christ dwells. The implication is profound: peace is not primarily an external accomplishment but an interior theophany.

Out of this triple alignment of faculties arises authentic **peace with God**: the "pura osservanza della legge e della volontà che Dio ha manifestato" — the pure observance of the law and the will that God has made manifest. Here Zorzi resolves a tension that had troubled medieval theology: How can human free will be preserved if God is the source of all peace, all concord, all motion of the soul toward the good? His answer, never fully systematized in this passage but implicit, is that observance of God's revealed will is not servility but the highest freedom—freedom from internal contradiction, from the war between flesh and spirit that Paul laments in Romans 7:14-25.

The section closes with Christological flourishes: Christ as *Principe della pace* (Isaiah 9:6), the angelic hymn of Luke 2:14 (*Gloria in altissimis Deo, et in terra pax hominibus bonae voluntatis*—"Peace on earth to men of good will"), and the city of celestial peace where God makes harmony reign, a reference synthesizing imagery from Job and the *Apocalypse*. The textual Latin interlude (lines 33–39) preserves the more technical formulation of the excludability principle:

> *"dissonans (vel in minimo) ad harmonicum concentum, et canoram suavitatem admitti non debet, nec potest. Sic et qui minimo peccato, vel aliqua in parte voluptati inserviunt, non possunt devenire ad regnum, cum sint servi peccati, nec ad pacem illam, quae a quibusdam sapientibus diffinitur esse tranquilla animi libertas."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus I (p. 2540 ed. Campanini)</cite>

This image is unambiguous and recalls the severity of Pythagorean and Platonic teaching: entrance into the Kingdom of Peace requires a soul entirely **free of dissonance** — even the smallest sin (*minimo peccato*) disqualifies one from the harmonious choir. The logical structure is strict: one cannot simultaneously be a slave to sin and possess that serene liberty of soul that peace constitutes. The *sapientes* here invoked are likely the Desert Fathers and early contemplatives whose apophthegms were being recovered and studied in the Renaissance, but the principle itself traces back through Augustine to Platonist teachings on the incompatibility of vice and the good.

---

## Concentus II: The Giver of Peace — God as Cosmic Tuner (§ 2)

### The Liturgical Invocation and the Problem of Grace

The second *concentus* turns from definition to source through a deliberately liturgical opening. Zorzi invokes the formula "Da nobis illam, quam mundus dare non potest, pacem" — "Grant us that peace which the world cannot give" — drawn from John 14:27 (*Pacem relinquo vobis, pacem meam do vobis*) and fixed in the Roman liturgy since the early medieval period. This invocation is not ornamental; it anchors the argument in the lived experience of the worshipping community. Peace is not an achievement (*possessio*) but a gift (*donum*) perpetually prayed for, perpetually granted. The very structure of liturgical petition acknowledges that peace transcends human willing.

Zorzi then develops a **three-dwelling cosmology** that maps spiritual states onto cosmic geography:

- **The *superna*** (the upper/celestial dwelling): perfectly ordered and filled with peace. This is the realm of the blessed, the choirs of angels, the immediate presence of God. It is characterized by perfect consonance—all wills united in the divine will, all intellects illuminated by divine Wisdom. In Neoplatonic terms, this is the realm of the One and the Nous; in Kabbalistic terms, the upper *Sefiroth* where the divine emanation is uninterrupted.

- **The *infima*** (the lower/infernal dwelling): where Job's "sempiternus horror" reigns (from Job 10:22, *terra tenebras et mortis umbra, et nulla est order*—the land of darkness and death's shadow, where no order exists), with no order, rest, or peace. This is the realm of the damned, of chaos, of complete discord between will and will, intellect and intellect. The horror is not primarily physical torment but the ontological condition of total fragmentation.

- **The *media*** (the middle dwelling): the human condition, a mixture of both. Humans alone occupy a liminal space where they may ascend or descend. They share with the angels the capacity for intellectual knowledge and moral choice; they share with beasts the needs and desires of embodied life. This intermediate status is both their glory and their peril.

The middle dwelling presents the soul with a **bivium** — a fork in the road — a trope inherited from Heracles at the Crossroads in Greek moral literature and Christianized in the *Psychomachia* of Prudentius and countless medieval moral treatises. Descent to the *inferna* is easy; ascent to the *superna* requires divine aid. The Virgilian echo of *facilimus est descensus Averni* (*Aeneid* VI.126—"the descent to Avernus is easy") is folded into a theological reading that transforms Virgil's observation about death into a principle of moral psychology: the ease of descent corresponds to moral dissolution (sin requires only the removal of will's resistance to passion), whereas the difficulty of ascent corresponds to the need for grace (virtue requires the positive assistance of divine power).

### The Central Metaphor: *Accordatore* and the Tuned Composite

The central metaphor of the passage then arrives in full development — **God as *Accordatore***, the tuner of a musical instrument. This image, deeply rooted in the Boethian *musica humana* tradition, receives from Zorzi an unprecedentedly detailed articulation:

> *"sicut instrumentum indiget modulatore, qui fibras aut fistulas aptet, et consonas reddat, et postea debita sonoritate moduletur, sic quoque nos tamquam instrumenta (et utinam bona) indigemus modulatore, qui nostros aptet nervos et fistulas, qui procul dubio sunt anima, atque corpus, animal nostrum, et spiritus, superior portio, et inferior, voluntas, et ratio, et tandem omnia cum ipso Deo concordet."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus II (p. 2541 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The *nervos et fistulas* — the strings and pipes of this divine instrument — are explicitly identified as:
- Soul and body (the two substances)
- The animal faculty (*animal nostrum*)
- The spirit (*spiritus*)
- The superior and inferior portions of the soul
- Will (*voluntas*)
- Reason (*ratio*)

The image has deep roots in Neoplatonic musical theology. Boethius, in his *De institutione musica*, had distinguished three kinds of music: the *musica universalis* (cosmic harmony), the *musica humana* (the harmony of the soul with the body), and the *musica instrumentalis* (audible music). Medieval and Renaissance thinkers had elaborated these categories, but Zorzi's innovation consists in making the human person not merely an instance of cosmic harmony but an instrument that requires **continuous divine tuning**. The *Accordatore* is not a distant creator who wound up the cosmic clock but an immediate, operative presence constantly maintaining the consonance of the human composite. Each moment of grace corresponds to a turn of the tuning peg; each moment of sin to a slackening of the string.

This has profound implications for Zorzi's theology of human nature. Unlike some Renaissance humanists who celebrated the independence and self-determination of the human will, Zorzi insists on what we might call "theurgic dependency": the human person is not self-tuning but must be continuously attuned by God. Yet this dependency is not annihilating but fulfilling—the tuned instrument produces the most beautiful music precisely because it has surrendered its autonomy to the tuner's art.

### Pauline Pneumatology and the Operative Cause

Two Pauline texts are cited in support of this vision:

- **Philippians 2:13**: "È Dio che suscita in noi il volere e l'agire" ("It is God who works in you both to will and to work for his good pleasure" — RSV *hyper tes eudokias*). Paul's language emphasizes that both the *thelein* (willing) and the *energein* (working, operative action) proceed from God. The human agent is not eliminated but is paradoxically most fully active when most thoroughly passive to divine action.

- **1 Corinthians 12:6**: "È Dio che opera tutto in tutti" ("It is the same God who inspires them all in every one" — the language of divine energeia, operative power, distributed throughout all).

From God as *Accordatore*, therefore, flow:

1. **The peace and concord of soul with body** — the cessation of the war between flesh and spirit.
2. **The subordination of the animal faculty to the spirit** — the appetites brought into obedience not through harsh suppression but through reorientation toward the divine good.
3. **The reciprocal correspondence of human minds** — the community of the Church understood not as a social contract but as an organism unified by the Spirit.
4. **The correspondence of our mind with the divine mind** — the ultimate peace of mystical union, *adhaerere Deo*.

Zorzi concludes the *concentus* with a comprehensive summary: "Di là proviene l'accordatura degli strumenti, di là ogni azione sonora e armoniosa, di là viene la serenità, la pace, la soavità e ogni bene" — "From there comes the tuning of the instruments, from there every sonorous and harmonious action, from there comes serenity, peace, sweetness, and every good." The image syntactically collapses the distinction between divine action and its effect: God's tuning *is* the peace; the tuning produces the sonority. There is no gap between cause and effect, no moment in which the soul is left unattended.

---

## Concentus III: The Sweetness of Peace and the Kabbalistic Excursus (§ 3)

### Encomia and the Attributes of Peace

The third *concentus* opens in a doxological register. Certain *sapientes* — "wise ones," likely referring to a tradition of patristic and monastic theology — declare that nothing in nature surpasses peace in beauty and sweetness. Zorzi provides a eulogistic enumeration of peace's attributes:

- **Serenità dell'anima** — serenity of the soul, transparency of consciousness
- **Semplicità di cuore** — simplicity of heart (a term from the Gospel tradition, recurring in Matthew 10:16's "nai serpentes et simplices sicut columbae")
- **Vincolo d'amore** — bond of love, the unifying force
- **Legame della carità** — link of charity, charity as the bond that holds all things together
- **Consolazione dello stato** — consolation of the soul's state or condition
- **Gioia delle case** — joy of households, domestic peace radiating from inner peace
- **Amica degli angeli** — friend of the angels, suggesting that peace makes the soul hospitable to angelic presence
- **Nemica dei demoni** — enemy of demons, the principle by which discord cannot enter a soul at peace
- **Gradita a Dio sopra ogni cosa** — pleasing to God above all things, the virtue that most honors the divine nature

This catalogue has its antecedents in patristic and scholastic encomia of peace—notably in Gregory of Nyssa's theological poetry and in the *Summa* of Thomas Aquinas, where peace (*pax*) figures as a fruit of the Holy Spirit. But Zorzi's enumeration emphasizes an aspect sometimes underemphasized in scholasticism: the **aesthetic dimension** of peace. Peace is not merely a moral achievement but something beautiful, luminous, and attractive—it makes the soul beautiful to the eyes of angels.

### Psalm 133: Fraternity and the Diffusion of Christ

The central scriptural text is **Psalm 133 (LXX 132)**: *"Ecce quam bonum et quam iucundum habitare fratres in unum."* — "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brothers to dwell together in unity!" 

Zorzi interprets the "fratelli" (brothers) with multiple simultaneous referents, all of which are valid in the contemplative economy:

1. **Soul and body in concord** — the two principal substances of the human being, "brothers" in their shared participation in the one person.
2. **All human beings together** — the eschatological vision of the gathered Church.
3. **A human being with his guardian angel** — the common medieval theology of angelic guardians as spiritual co-dwellers.

When this concord obtains, Christ (whose name in the *Canticle of Canticles* is *unguentum effusum*, from Song 1:3—"Your name is as oil poured out") diffuses himself over those assembled. The image is sacramental: just as anointing oil runs downward in sheets, so too does Christ's presence flow into and throughout those gathered in unity. The reference to the *Canticle* introduces an erotic-mystical register—the language of consummation and penetration—that underscores the reality and intimacy of the indwelling.

### The Anointing Oil: Aaron, Christ, and Priestly Mediation

Psalm 133:2 describes the oil as descending "upon the beard of Aaron" (*super barbam Aaron*). In the historical-typological reading, this refers to Aaron's consecration as high priest (Exodus 29). But Zorzi performs a Christological re-reading. The oil that descends upon Aaron is read as the priestly mediation of divine *charismata* — the gifts of grace and the Spirit. The high-priestly unction that flows from Christ (the ultimate *archiereus*) descends through the ordained ministry to the people.

Zorzi notes that the oil descends to the *ora vestimenti*, the "edge" or "hem" of the vestment. This garment is interpreted as the vestment "Christ acquired and wove for us" (*quam sibi et nobis dum adquisivit et texuit*) — a striking typological reading that binds together Christology, sacerdotalism, and the healing of the wounded soul. The garment represents both Christ's humanity (the linen of his flesh) and the covering of grace that conceals human sin from the divine judgment. The oil seeping down to its hem signifies that grace extends to the lowest, most damaged, and most hidden parts of human nature—nothing is left unanointed.

This Incarnational theology emphasizes the material, corporeal dimensions of redemption. The *ora* is the "border," the point of contact between the sacred and the ordinary. Grace, for Zorzi, does not dissolve matter into spirit but penetrates and sanctifies the material itself.

### The Dew of Hermon: Kabbalistic Numerology and the Hidden Name

The second image of Psalm 133, the *rugiada dell'Hermon che scende sul monte di Sion* (v. 3)—"the dew of Hermon descending upon the mountain of Zion"—triggers the chapter's most distinctly **Kabbalistic passage**. Zorzi interprets:

- **Hermon** etymologically as *anatema della tristezza* — "the curse of sadness" or "separation from joy." The interpretation is drawn from Jerome's *Liber interpretationis hebraicorum nominum*, where *Hermon* is glossed as *anathema tristitiae* or *consecration of sadness* (Hebrew *herem*, curse/anathema, + *on*, or *hem*, literally "hotness," metaphorically passion/sadness). The mountain of Hermon is thus figured as the high place of sorrow, the place where tears fall—a geography of lamentation.

- **Sion** as *specola* — watchtower, lookout, the elevated place from which all is seen and guarded. Making the mountain a figure for those who govern and spiritually oversee others. The high places of wisdom and authority.

- **Tal** (טַל), the Hebrew word for dew, as bearing a hidden numerical significance through the science of *gematria*:

> *"ros qui Hebraice (טַל) tal dicitur, per viam numerorum cognomen importat magni nominis reconditi, a quo fluunt omnia."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus III (p. 2544 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The gloss on *tal* constitutes a technical **Kabbalistic exegesis**. *Gematria* is the system whereby each Hebrew letter is assigned a numerical value (aleph = 1, bet = 2, ... tav = 400). The word *tal* breaks down as follows:

- ט (*tet*) = 9
- ל (*lamed*) = 30
- **Total = 39**

This numerical value of 39 is identical to the gematria of the phrase *YHWH Echad* — "The Lord is One" — the core declaration of the *Shema Yisrael* (Deuteronomy 6:4). Specifically:

- י (*yud*) = 10
- ה (*he*) = 5
- ו (*vav*) = 6
- ה (*he*) = 5
- א (*aleph*) = 1
- ח (*het*) = 8
- ד (*daleth*) = 4
- **Total = 39**

Zorzi thus encodes the dew of Hermon as a **cipher for the divine Name** and its *cognomen* — a Kabbalistic technical term likely referring to the explicit pronunciation substitute for the Tetragrammaton (such as *Adonai*, "Lord") or possibly to the sefirotic principle through which the Name is made knowable. In Kabbalistic theology, the hidden Name (*Shem* in its most occult sense) is unknowable and unpronounceable, but it makes itself accessible through grades and *cognomina* (names, attributes) that correspond to the *Sefiroth* on the Tree of Life.

The passage continues with breathtaking theological density:

> *"per unguentum indicatur, quod habeamus influxum nominis, quod proprie est unguentum effusum, et per rorem influxum cognominis, adeo, ut nihil desit habitantibus simul in pace, et unitate."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus III (p. 2544 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The theological arithmetic is precise: the anointing oil figures the *influxus* (the downward flowing, the emanation) of the divine Name itself; the dew figures the *influxus* of the *cognomen* — the mode by which the hidden is made knowable. Together, they ensure that those "dwelling together in peace and unity" lack nothing. The dew, being finer, lighter, and more penetrating than oil, suggests an even more intimate influx—the grace not merely descending upon externals but seeping into every pore of consciousness.

The phrase "the theologians called the esoteric ones" (*theologi esoterici* / *secretiores theologi*) is Zorzi's habitual designation for **Kabbalistic authorities**. In the context of Renaissance Christian Kabbalah (which flourished in circles influenced by Pico della Mirandola), these "esoteric theologians" were understood to possess knowledge of the divine architecture hidden beneath Scripture. Zorzi is claiming that the Kabbalists, through their mastery of gematria and the sefirotic system, have preserved the ancient Hebraic wisdom that the patriarchs and prophets possessed—a wisdom not contrary to Christian faith but fulfilling and deepening it.

The movement is **characteristically Zorzian**: a literal/typological reading of a psalm verse is elevated into a sefirotic or onomastic register without abandoning the literal sense. One reads *psalm 133 literally as about actual human peace*, typologically as about the mediation of grace through priesthood, and esoterically as about the flow of the divine Name through its *cognomina* into human consciousness. All three readings are simultaneously valid and mutually reinforcing.

---

## Closing Ascent: The Four-Stage Eschatological Ladder

Zorzi draws the chapter to a close with a four-stage **eschatological ladder** that maps the soul's ascent:

1. **Peace** (*pax*) — the concordia of all the soul's faculties and of the soul with God. This is the threshold, the reestablishment of internal order after the chaos of sin.

2. **Blessing** (*benedictio*) — the gift through which beatitude is reached. Those entering the Kingdom of Glory are called "*benedicti* Patris mei" by the Prince (Matthew 25:34: *"Venite benedicti Patris mei, possidete paratum vobis regnum a constitutione mundi"*—"Come, you blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world"). The movement from peace to blessing suggests a deepening: peace is the condition, blessing is the divine word that consecrates and ratifies that condition.

3. **Life** (*vita*) — eternal beatitude, not merely as duration but as the fullness of existence (*plenitude essendi*). Having been at peace and having received blessing, the soul enters into that life which neither moths nor thieves can destroy.

4. **Union** (*unio*) — the subject of the following section, toward which the entire harmonic edifice of *De Harmonia Mundi* ascends. Zorzi promises to approach it "*nella misura del lecito*" — "to the measure that is lawful," that is, respecting the necessary limits of discursive language and the veiling of the divine mysteries—and *"per quanto ce lo consentirà la debolezza del nostro modesto ingegno"* — "inasmuch as the weakness of our modest intelligence will permit." This rhetorical *captatio benevolentiae* signals the approach of the work's **mystical apex**, the union of the soul with God that is the fruition of all harmonic ascent.

The four-stage ladder is itself a harmony: peace-blessing-life-union as four notes in an ascending scale, each dependent on the prior, each leading to the next. The image suggests that eschatology is not merely a distant goal but a process already underway within the soul that has awakened to the divine *Accordatore*.

---

## Significance within *De Harmonia Mundi*

This *modulo* marks a **pivotal transition** in the *Canticum Tertium*. The earlier books treated cosmic, celestial, and natural harmony; here the locus of harmony shifts decisively to the moral and mystical interior. The musical metaphor, which throughout the work has operated cosmologically, is here fully **internalized**: the human person is the instrument, God is the tuner, and peace is the sounding of all faculties in perfect consonance.

The structure of the argument moves from definition to agency to experience: we are told what peace is (Concentus I), who gives it and how (Concentus II), and what it tastes like and how it connects to the hidden divine Name (Concentus III). Each *concentus* adds a layer of depth, moving from the rational-ethical to the mystical-sacramental to the esoteric-Kabbalistic.

The Kabbalistic reading of *tal* is not ornamental but **structural**: it grounds the promise of Psalm 133 in the hidden architecture of the divine Name, ensuring that the peace celebrated by the Psalmist is not merely ethical sentiment but an **ontological reality** — a real influx of the divine Name into those who dwell in unity. The gematria of *tal* (39) equaling *YHWH Echad* (39) guarantees that the dew falling on Zion is literally the descending of the divine Name itself in its unifying power. This is not metaphor but, in Kabbalistic understanding, literal numerical-mystical fact.

The chapter thus prepares the ground for the treatment of *unio* that follows, the **summit** toward which the entire harmonic edifice of the *De Harmonia Mundi* ascends. One cannot approach union until the soul is at peace; one cannot be at peace until one ceases to resist the divine *Accordatore*; one cannot cease to resist without the influx of the very Name of God. The theology is airtight, the mysticism grounded in the mathematical precision of gematria, the whole vision unified by the image of the divinely-tuned instrument producing, at last, the single pure note of union.
